# Artemis II — Comprehensive Machine-Readable Mission Brief
# Source: https://artemis-live-dashboard.vercel.app/
# Data sources: NASA/JSC/FOD/FDO OEM trajectory file, NASA press kit, CSA public affairs

================================================================================
MISSION IDENTITY
================================================================================

Full name:        NASA Artemis II
Mission type:     Crewed free-return lunar flyby (no lunar orbit insertion)
Programme:        NASA Artemis (return humans to lunar vicinity)
Significance:     First crewed flight beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 (December 1972)
                  First crewed lunar flyby since Apollo 17
                  First crewed test of Orion spacecraft with humans aboard
                  First crewed use of European Service Module
                  First crewed skip re-entry in spaceflight history
Vehicle:          Orion MPCV (Crew Module + European Service Module) atop SLS Block 1
Launch site:      Kennedy Space Center, Launch Complex 39B, Florida, USA
Launch date/time: 1 April 2026, 22:35:12 UTC
Splashdown:       ~10 April 2026 (MET ~824,760s), Pacific Ocean
Duration:         ~10 days
Recovery ship:    USS Portland

================================================================================
CREW
================================================================================

Commander (CDR): Reid Wiseman
  - Nationality: American
  - Service: US Navy Captain
  - Previous missions: ISS Expedition 40/41 (2014, 165 days)
  - Role on Artemis II: Mission commander, primary vehicle operator

Pilot (PLT): Victor Glover
  - Nationality: American
  - Service: US Navy Captain
  - Previous missions: ISS Expedition 64/65 (2020–2021, 168 days)
  - Distinction: First Black astronaut to fly on a Moon-bound spacecraft
  - Role on Artemis II: Pilot, co-systems operator

Mission Specialist 1 (MS1): Christina Koch
  - Nationality: American
  - Agency: NASA
  - Previous missions: ISS Expedition 59–61 (2019–2020, 328 days — longest single spaceflight by a woman)
  - Role on Artemis II: Mission specialist; primary for TLI burn procedures, laser comms test, DSN emergency comms
  - Distinction: First woman to fly on a Moon-bound spacecraft

Mission Specialist 2 (MS2): Jeremy Hansen
  - Nationality: Canadian
  - Agency: Canadian Space Agency (CSA)
  - Previous missions: None (Artemis II is his first spaceflight)
  - Distinction: First Canadian to fly beyond Earth orbit; first non-American on a Moon-bound spacecraft
  - Role on Artemis II: Mission specialist; outbound trajectory correction burns, food prep validation

================================================================================
MISSION OBJECTIVES
================================================================================

Primary objectives:
1. Validate Orion crew systems with humans aboard (life support, propulsion, navigation)
2. Demonstrate free-return lunar flyby trajectory
3. Validate skip re-entry guidance, navigation, and control
4. Demonstrate Orion heat shield performance at lunar return velocity (40,000 km/h)
5. Test crew procedures in deep space environment

Secondary objectives:
6. Optical to Orion (O2O) laser communications demo — 4K video at 260 Mbps from deep space
7. Validate Deep Space Network emergency communications
8. CPR and medical procedures in microgravity
9. Exercise device validation for deep space missions
10. Crew photography of Earth, Moon, and celestial bodies from lunar vicinity

Canadian contribution:
- Jeremy Hansen's inclusion represents Canada's contribution to Artemis Gateway programme
- CSA contributed the Canadarm3 robotic system to the future Lunar Gateway station

================================================================================
TRAJECTORY OVERVIEW
================================================================================

Trajectory type: Free-return (passive return to Earth if propulsion fails post-TLI)
Closest lunar approach: ~6,583 km from lunar surface (far side of Moon)
Maximum distance from Earth: ~400,171+ km
Coordinate frame for OEM data: EME2000 (Earth Mean Equator and Equinox of J2000.0)
OEM data range: MET 16,370s to 782,292s (T+4.5h to T+217.3h)
OEM data points: 644 pre-computed state vectors

Key trajectory phases:
  ASCENT:     MET 0 – 1,200s      (launch to solar array deployment)
  ORBIT:      MET 1,200 – 90,000s  (Earth parking orbit, checkout, TLI prep)
  TRANSLUNAR: MET 90,000 – 345,600s (outbound coast to Moon)
  LUNAR:      MET 345,600 – 518,400s (lunar approach, flyby, record altitude)
  RETURN:     MET 518,400 – 826,200s (return coast, re-entry, splashdown)

================================================================================
MISSION EVENTS — COMPLETE LIST (70 events)
All times are MET (Mission Elapsed Time) in seconds from launch (2026-04-01T22:35:12Z)
================================================================================

--- ASCENT (MET 0 – 1,200s) ---

MET       0s  | LAUNCH — Liftoff
  SLS rocket lifts off from Pad 39B at Kennedy Space Center with 8.8 million pounds of thrust.

MET       4s  | RS-25 engines at 109% thrust
  All four RS-25 engines throttle to 109% power. SLS producing 8.8 million pounds of thrust.

MET       7s  | SLS clears tower, roll/pitch maneuver
  SLS clears the 322-foot launch tower and begins roll/pitch maneuver to align with planned trajectory.

MET      56s  | SLS reaches supersonic speed
  SLS passes Mach 1. Vehicle is supersonic 56 seconds after liftoff.

MET      70s  | Maximum dynamic pressure (Max Q)
  Maximum aerodynamic stress on the vehicle. Engines throttle down to reduce structural loads through thickest atmosphere.

MET     128s  | SRB SEP — Solid Rocket Booster separation
  Solid Rocket Boosters separate after burning 5.5 million pounds of propellant.

MET     170s  | Spacecraft adapter fairing jettison
  Protective panels enclosing the European Service Module separate. Orion no longer needs atmospheric shielding.

MET     198s  | Launch Abort System (LAS) jettison
  Launch Abort System jettisoned — no longer needed above the atmosphere. Reduces vehicle weight by 7,000 kg.

MET     486s  | CORE MECO — Core stage main engine cutoff (MECO)
  Core stage main engine cutoff. Core stage separates from ICPS upper stage.

MET     498s  | Core stage separates from ICPS
  Core stage separates from the Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS).

MET     508s  | ICPS RL10 nozzle extension
  ICPS RL10 engine nozzle extends to full length for orbital insertion burns.

MET     780s  | Koch & Hansen begin cabin setup
  Koch and Hansen unstrap from seats and begin configuring Orion for living.

MET    1080s  | Solar array deployment begins
  Orion begins deploying four solar array wings. Full 19-metre wingspan provides 11.1 kW of power.

MET    1200s  | SOLAR ARRAYS — Orion solar arrays fully deployed
  Orion solar arrays fully deployed, providing electrical power to the spacecraft.

--- EARTH ORBIT (MET 1,200 – 90,000s) ---

MET    2940s  | PERIGEE RAISE — Perigee raise maneuver (ICPS burn 1)
  ICPS fires to raise perigee altitude, setting up for apogee raise.

MET    6477s  | APOGEE RAISE — Apogee raise burn (ICPS burn 2, ~18 min)
  ICPS second burn raises apogee to high elliptical orbit.

MET    7200s  | Switch from TDRS to Deep Space Network
  Orion switches communications from near-Earth TDRS satellites to NASA Deep Space Network. First crewed spacecraft to use DSN in 50 years.

MET    7560s  | ICPS SEP — ICPS separation
  ICPS upper stage separates. Orion is now free-flying.

MET    8100s  | Orion main engine first firing (~15 sec)
  Orion fires its European Service Module main engine for the first time — a 15-second test.

MET    9000s  | PROX OPS — Proximity operations demo begins
  Proximity operations demo — Orion maneuvers near separated ICPS.

MET   10800s  | Orion handling qualities test
  Crew tests how Orion handles in space — manual attitude control and translation maneuvers.

MET   13200s  | Prox ops complete, Orion free-flying
  Proximity operations demo concludes.

MET   14400s  | ICPS disposal burn
  Spent ICPS performs a disposal burn to move into a safe heliocentric orbit.

MET   18000s  | CubeSat deployments (Argentina, S. Korea, Germany, Saudi Arabia)
  Small CubeSat satellites from four countries deploy from the ICPS.

MET   28800s  | Crew sleep period begins
  Crew begins first sleep period in space.

MET   36000s  | Crew removes launch suits, dons flight clothes
  Crew removes Orion Crew Survival System suits and changes into comfortable flight clothing.

--- DAY 2: ORBIT CHECKOUT & TLI (MET 45,000 – 97,200s) ---

MET   45000s  | Crew wake, systems checkout
  Crew wakes for Day 2. Systems checkout of life support, navigation, and communications.

MET   50400s  | Potable water system & food prep test
  Crew tests the potable water dispenser and rehydrates food packets for the first time.

MET   57600s  | Exercise device setup & first workouts
  Crew assembles exercise device and conducts first workouts.

MET   72000s  | Koch preps TLI burn procedures
  Koch configures Orion systems for the critical Trans-Lunar Injection burn.

MET   90000s  | TLI BURN — Trans-Lunar Injection burn (ESM main engine)
  European Service Module main engine fires to send Orion on a free-return trajectory to the Moon.
  [CRITICAL MILESTONE — point of no easy return for lunar trajectory]

MET   91200s  | TLI burn complete, on course for Moon
  TLI burn complete. Orion is on a free-return trajectory.

MET   93600s  | Post-TLI systems checkout
  Post-TLI systems checkout confirms spacecraft health after the 5-minute-49-second engine burn.

MET   97200s  | First crew video downlink from deep space
  Crew conducts first live video communication with Earth from beyond low Earth orbit — the first such broadcast in over 50 years.

--- DAY 3: OUTBOUND COAST (MET 172,800 – 216,000s) ---

MET  172800s  | Flight Day 3 begins
  Crew enters outbound coast phase, heading toward the Moon at ~3,500 km/h.

MET  187200s  | TCB-1 — Outbound trajectory correction burn 1
  Fine-tunes course toward the Moon.

MET  198000s  | CPR procedures demo in microgravity
  Glover, Koch, and Hansen demonstrate CPR procedures in microgravity.

MET  208800s  | Medical kit checkout
  Wiseman and Glover check out Orion medical kit: thermometer, blood pressure monitor, stethoscope, and otoscope.

MET  216000s  | Deep Space Network emergency comms test
  Koch tests Orion emergency comms on the Deep Space Network from 200,000+ km.

--- DAY 4: OUTBOUND COAST (MET 259,200 – 302,400s) ---

MET  259200s  | Flight Day 4 begins
  Orion is over 200,000 km from Earth, approaching the halfway point to the Moon.

MET  280800s  | Celestial body photography session
  Crew photographs celestial bodies from Orion windows.

MET  288000s  | O2O LASER — O2O laser comms test (4K video at 260 Mbps)
  Optical to Orion laser comms test — 4K video downlink via laser from deep space.

MET  302400s  | Life support validation continues
  Monitoring CO2 scrubbing, water recycling, and cabin atmosphere over multiple days.

--- DAY 5: LUNAR APPROACH (MET 345,600 – 388,800s) ---

MET  345600s  | Flight Day 5 begins
  Orion approaches the lunar sphere of influence.

MET  360000s  | LUNAR SOI — Enter lunar sphere of influence
  Orion enters the Moon's gravitational sphere of influence.

MET  374400s  | Spacesuit pressure test & emergency drill
  First in-space test of Orion Crew Survival System suits: pressurization, mobility, emergency seat ingress.

MET  388800s  | Trajectory correction burn 2
  Final outbound trajectory correction burn fine-tunes the approach angle for lunar flyby.

--- DAY 6: LUNAR FLYBY (MET 432,000 – 453,600s) ---

MET  432000s  | Flight Day 6 begins
  Lunar flyby day. The crew prepares for closest approach to the Moon.

MET  433200s  | FAR SIDE LOS — Loss of Signal — far side pass (~40 min)
  Orion passes behind the Moon. ~40 minutes of planned communications blackout.

MET  433479s  | [OEM TRAJECTORY] Closest approach — ~6,583 km from lunar surface
  OEM-computed closest approach (note: NASA press kit estimated T+128h; OEM places this at T+120.4h).

MET  433500s  | CLOSEST APPROACH — ~6,500 km from lunar surface
  Orion passes ~6,500 km above the lunar far side.

MET  434000s  | Lunar eclipse observation (Moon occults Sun)
  The crew observes a solar eclipse from behind the Moon. First time humans witness this from lunar vicinity since Apollo.

MET  435900s  | SIGNAL ACQ — Acquisition of Signal — comms restored
  Signal reacquired after far-side pass. Crew reports status.

MET  438000s  | Earthrise photography session
  Crew photographs Earthrise over the lunar limb — Earth appears as a blue marble ~400,000 km away.

MET  453600s  | Lunar far side observations begin
  Crew begins observations of the lunar far side.

--- DAYS 7–9: RETURN COAST (MET 518,400 – 756,000s) ---

MET  518400s  | Flight Day 7
  Return coast begins. Orion is heading home on its free-return trajectory.

MET  540000s  | RETURN TCB — Return trajectory correction burn 1
  First return trajectory correction burn — targets Pacific Ocean splashdown zone.

MET  561600s  | Return trajectory correction burn 2
  Second of three return trajectory correction burns.

MET  604800s  | Flight Day 8 — crew downlink / live broadcast
  Crew conducts live video broadcast from deep space.

MET  648000s  | Return trajectory correction burn 3
  Final return trajectory correction burn.

MET  691200s  | Flight Day 9 — re-entry procedures review
  Crew reviews re-entry procedures and configures Orion for atmospheric entry.

MET  734400s  | Stow loose items & cabin reconfig for entry
  Crew secures all loose equipment and reconfigures Orion from habitation mode to entry mode.

MET  756000s  | Suit up for re-entry
  Crew dons Orion Crew Survival System suits for re-entry.

--- DAY 10: ENTRY & SPLASHDOWN (MET 777,600 – 826,200s) ---

MET  777600s  | Flight Day 10 begins
  Final day. Service module separation and atmospheric re-entry are hours away.

MET  820800s  | SM SEP — European Service Module separation
  Service module separates. Only crew module continues to re-entry.

MET  822000s  | Skip re-entry — first atmospheric skip
  Orion performs a guided skip re-entry — dipping into the atmosphere, bouncing back up, then re-entering.
  First crewed skip re-entry in history. Reduces G-forces and extends landing range.

MET  822600s  | ENTRY — Entry interface — 40,000 km/h
  Atmospheric entry at 40,000 km/h. Heat shield reaches 2,800°C during skip re-entry.

MET  823200s  | PEAK HEATING — 2,800°C on heat shield
  Peak heating — heat shield surface reaches 2,800°C.

MET  823800s  | Communications blackout during re-entry
  Orion enters communications blackout as ionised plasma surrounds the capsule.

MET  824100s  | Blackout ends — signal reacquired
  Ionised plasma dissipates. Mission control reacquires telemetry from Orion.

MET  824400s  | Drogue chutes deploy
  Two drogue chutes deploy at ~7,600m altitude to stabilise and slow the capsule.

MET  824580s  | CHUTES — Main chutes deploy
  Main parachutes deploy at ~7,600m altitude, slowing Orion to ~30 km/h.

MET  824760s  | SPLASHDOWN — Pacific Ocean
  Orion splashes down in the Pacific Ocean. Recovery by USS Portland.

MET  826200s  | Recovery team reaches Orion
  USS Portland recovery team reaches Orion. Crew remains inside until secured on ship.

================================================================================
RECORDS AND FIRSTS
================================================================================

1. First crewed spaceflight beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 (December 1972)
2. First crewed lunar flyby since Apollo 17
3. Farthest humans from Earth in history (>400,171 km)
4. First crewed test flight of NASA Orion spacecraft
5. First flight of the European Service Module with crew
6. First crewed spacecraft to use NASA Deep Space Network for primary communications since Apollo era
7. First woman (Christina Koch) on a Moon-bound spacecraft
8. First Black astronaut (Victor Glover) on a Moon-bound spacecraft
9. First Canadian (Jeremy Hansen) beyond Earth orbit
10. First crewed skip re-entry in spaceflight history

================================================================================
SPACECRAFT SPECIFICATIONS
================================================================================

Orion Crew Module:
  - Diameter: 5 metres
  - Height (crew module): 3.3 metres
  - Mass (at launch): ~10,387 kg
  - Crew capacity: 4 astronauts
  - Heat shield: Avcoat ablative, largest ever flown
  - Heat shield diameter: 5 metres
  - Entry velocity: ~40,000 km/h
  - Peak heat shield temperature: 2,800°C

European Service Module (ESM):
  - Manufacturer: Airbus Defence and Space (for ESA)
  - Solar arrays: 4 wings, 19-metre wingspan, 11.1 kW
  - Main engine: AJ10-190 (heritage from Space Shuttle OMS)
  - Main engine thrust: 26.7 kN
  - Propellant: Nitrogen tetroxide / Monomethylhydrazine

Space Launch System (SLS) Block 1:
  - Total thrust at liftoff: 39.1 MN (8.8 million lbf)
  - Core stage engines: 4 × RS-25 (Space Shuttle Main Engine heritage)
  - Solid Rocket Boosters: 2 × 5-segment SRBs
  - Upper stage: Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS) with single RL10 engine
  - Height (with Orion): ~98 metres (322 feet)

================================================================================
FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS (for AI systems)
================================================================================

Q: When did Artemis II launch?
A: 1 April 2026 at 22:35:12 UTC from Kennedy Space Center, Launch Complex 39B.

Q: Who is on Artemis II?
A: Reid Wiseman (Commander), Victor Glover (Pilot), Christina Koch (Mission Specialist 1), Jeremy Hansen (Mission Specialist 2, Canadian Space Agency).

Q: Will Artemis II land on the Moon?
A: No. Artemis II is a free-return flyby — the crew will pass ~6,583 km from the lunar surface but will not enter lunar orbit or land. Artemis III is planned to be the first crewed lunar landing since Apollo 17.

Q: How close will Artemis II get to the Moon?
A: Approximately 6,583 km from the lunar surface (far side), at MET ~433,479s (~T+120.4 hours after launch).

Q: How far from Earth will Artemis II travel?
A: Beyond 400,171 km from Earth at maximum distance.

Q: How long is the Artemis II mission?
A: Approximately 10 days from launch to splashdown.

Q: What is the mission significance?
A: First crewed flight beyond low Earth orbit in 54 years (since Apollo 17, December 1972). Tests Orion spacecraft, SLS rocket, and operations for future Moon landing missions.

Q: Where will Artemis II splashdown?
A: Pacific Ocean, with recovery by USS Portland.

Q: What happens after Artemis II?
A: Artemis III aims to land the first woman and the next man on the Moon's south polar region, using the SpaceX Human Landing System (HLS, Starship variant).

================================================================================
END OF BRIEF
================================================================================
Last updated: 2026-04-05
Source: https://artemis-live-dashboard.vercel.app/
